Always check for reentrancy vulnerabilities before external calls. Use Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern.
tx.origin should never be used for authorization. Use msg.sender instead.
Integer overflow and underflow are still critical in older Solidity versions. Use SafeMath or Solidity 0.8+.
Oracles can be manipulated. Never rely on a single price source for critical logic.
Flash loans can change the state of your protocol within a single transaction. Design accordingly.
Access control misconfiguration is one of the most common and costly vulnerabilities.
Always validate cross-chain message senders. Bridge exploits often come from missing validation.
Delegatecall is powerful and dangerous. The callee can modify the caller's storage.
Frontrunning is real. Sensitive operations should use commit-reveal schemes or slippage protection.
Read the protocol's own documentation and compare it with the code. Discrepancies are often bugs.
Low severity findings compound. Multiple low issues can create a critical vulnerability.
Never assume tokens behave like standard ERC-20. Fee-on-transfer and rebasing tokens break assumptions.
Signature replay attacks are common. Always include chainId and nonce in signed messages.
Rounding errors in favor of users can drain a protocol over time.
Audit your dependencies too. A vulnerable library can compromise a secure contract.
Emergency pause mechanisms are good. Emergency withdraw functions without access control are not.
Test edge cases — zero values, max values, and empty arrays often reveal hidden bugs.
Events don't have to be emitted for state changes to happen. But missing events make monitoring harder.

Measure before optimizing.
Delete unused code — dead code becomes attack surface.
Simple systems are easier to secure.
Learn the protocol before building on top of it.
Read incident reports; they're free lessons.
Never ignore unusual behavior in logs.
Your monitoring should detect failures before users do.
Redundancy is cheaper than downtime.
Technical debt accumulates interest.
Every system eventually fails — design for recovery.
Good engineers prevent problems; great engineers prevent classes of problems.
Understand how things break, not just how they work.
Assume APIs will fail and handle errors gracefully.
Make the secure path the easiest path.
